CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The “ Imish ”’ Times is attempting to make a double-
faced bogey out of Mr. Edward Martyn’s Party; one
‘face of the bogey is intended as a warning to the Big
Brother and the Garrison, and the other is put up

- with a view to frightening the Catholic clergy at what
may be expected to follow Home Rule! AIf is very
smart, isn’t he? The extraordinary harangue of
Alderman Cole—one of Mr. Martyn’s most prominent
lieutenants—at the Dublin Corporation during the
course of the debate, raised by Dr. McWalter’s motion
protesting against the French Government outrage on

e Church, is grist for the mill of the libeller of

. Canon McInerney. Arnott’s paper attempts to make a
bogey out of it in the hope of its terrifying the Irish
Catholic clergy.. Innocent AIf is stupid enough to
think that that is cunning!” In the course of a leading

i?‘ icle,- Arnott’s paper of Saturday, referring to Mr.
s and Mir. Sweotman’s Party, said:—In-

fluenced to a striking degree by the doctrines of Con-
tinental Socialism, it has begun to assert a freedom of
action on questions of religion and- education which
constitutes a startling innovation in Irish affairs.’”
What! does Arnott’s Zmes wish to suggest a sort of
impressionist sketch of Mr. Edward Martyn as a toy
Ivish Clemenceau, and of Mr. John Sweetman as a lath-
and-plaster M. Combes! Let it take care that it does
not give either of these two very mild and law-abiding
gentlemen a cause of action for libel as it gave Canon
McInerney!  Further down in its leading artic'e
Arnott’s paper, referring to Alderman Cole’s extra-
ordinary remarks, said :— Tho latter (Alderman Cole)
boldly declared that ‘he would not raise his voice in
opposition to the declared will of the French people in
regard to matters which affected the French in their
capacity as laymen.” We can readily see in such lan-
guage a reaction from the influences which have
hitherto put a check upon freedom of thought in Ire-
'and. ‘We can find in it also an explanation of the
doubts which have begun to assail the Irish Roman
Catholic Hierarchy concerning the future of this
country under a Home Rule Government.””

What does Arnott’s paper know about “ the doubts,”’
or rather we should say the alleged doubts, of the
Catholic Hierarchy? Arnott’s paper must have indeed
as poor an opinion of the Irish Catholic clergy in
general as it once had—to its great cost and humilia-
tion—of Canon McInerney in particular, if it thinks
that the bombast of a person like Alderman Cole, lieu-
tenant in Mr. Edward Martyn’s awkward squad, will
prejudice the Irish Hierarchy against Home Rule.

Of course it is only to be expected that the “ Zrish ’’
Times will make as much England’s Faithful Garrison
capital as it can out of Mr. Martyn’s Party. In the
absence of outrages against ‘andlords, and the maim-
ing of cattle, we are not surprised to find England’s
Faithful Garrison’s principal newspaper clutching at
the coat tails of Mr. Edward Martyn and Mr. John
Sweetman. We are glad to find the Irish T4mes work-
ing Alderman Cole’s remarks for all they are worth ; in
doing so it has rendered a good service to the National
cause, though, needless to say, quite unintentionally.
It is well that these sort of things shou'd break out on
the surface as early as possible, and that when they do,
that special attention should be called to them.

Mr. Stephen Gwynn, M.P., gave Mr. Martyn’s Party
a bad quarter of an hour at an U.L.L. meeting in
Dublin. ‘We forget exactly what the theoretical pro-
gramme of the U.I.L. is, but what the U.L.L. needs
to do is to practise nation-building.: It -has the Irish
language on its programme, we believe, but we have
never heard of an Irish class in connection with any
branch of the United Irish League. What practical
work has it done, as an organisation for Irish manu-
facturing industry? No doubt a good many ‘‘ support-
Irish-industry ’’ speeches have been delivered from its
platform; but the U.LL., as far as our knowledge
goes, in no part of the country endeavours to tackle
many of the nation-building problems. Whatever its
theoretic programme may be, the U.I.L. remains
largely in the old exclusive.and belated political rut.
No doubt Mr. Gwynn as regards the language, Mr.
Boland as regards.industry, and' Mr. McVeigh as re-
gards the fight for Tolerance, do each a man’s share,
but these men are unfortunately exceptions, and they
probably see as well as we do that, generally speak

. the United Irish League is principally a. polif

©

machine for producing “eloquent’’ speeches, unanimous
resolutions, torch-light processions, and subscriptions
from “the priests and people.”” Wo don’t know how
the U.LL. stands as an organised body, but however
organised it may be, it requires a good deal of Irish
3 iife put into it. There is nothing to prevent the
U.LL. developing itself as an Irish-Ireland body with
abtracting power over Irish Irelanders; but as a
. matter of fact it has not done so, and so Irish Ire-
landers keep aloof from it, or else join it merely in their
capacity as politicians. Mr. Gwynn sees clearly the
absurdity of the theories of Mr. Edward Martyn's
party; he cannot be blind to the barrenness in practical
work of his own concerning many dopartments of
nation-making work.

In the course of a curious case tried at Olones
Quarter Sessions, Judge Craig said:—" They (the
Bungs) won’t make any distinction at all if they can
get money. These public-houses are carried on under
the most wicked and ruthless system. If they can get
money that is all they care for. They don’t care if it
comes from hell if they can rake it into their tills,
and they care nothing whatever if they are the means
of sending to hell, body and soul, the people from
whom they get it. But there is no use in my talking
so long as the public fceling of the country allows it.
Still I do talk when cases like this come before me.”
This may appear strong language to some people. It
was used in connection with a case in which a minor
sued through his mother to recover £3 balance of
wages alleged to be due to him.  The minor, John
M‘Kernan, was a strip of a lad between fiftcen and
sixteen years of age, and he was engaged by a Mr.
Edward Maguire, Crowfort, Scotchouse, at £7 10s. for
a half-year. This lad was allowed to run up a hill at
a bungery owned by a brother of Maguire’s!  Judge
Craig said:—“It is a scandalous thing to allow a boy
like this to run up an account in a public-houso,
especially when it belonged to his employer’s brother.”
The manager of the bungery, a Mr. Hugh P. Barkey,
admitted in cross-examination that on the 15th August
this bit of a lad took half a pint of whiskey away with
him! Besides the drink charged in the bill, the slip
of a boy took drink in the bungery that he paid for.
The lad spent money in the house on the 15th August
in addition to taking a half pint of whiskey away with
him. Judge Craig said that he would not allow one
farthing for the goods got in the shop, and gave a
decree for £1 3s. 9d., with 3s. expenses. Is this a new
grievance for pious, moral and philanthropic Mr.
Bung?

Several Dublin T.C.’s, and several who vainly tried
to be T.C.’s, being desirous of thanking their sup-
porters—and, at the <gme time, but only as a matter
of detail, advertising themselves at the rate of nothing
an inch—sought to trespass on the “ valuable space’’ of
the Bvening Telegraph. But these are hard times, and
the “free pass’’ list is evidently suspended at Prince’s
St. Pink has to look carefully after the gate money
these times, and so Pink warned the T.C.’s and would-
be T.C.’s off the grass. “All such communications,”
declared Pink, “should be addressed to our advertis-
ing manager.” Pink, after all, is not so green as some
of the frugal T.C.’s and would-be T.C.’s imagined.

The Statist is an English sixpenny weekly economic
journal. In its issue of January 12th we find an
article entitled “ Bureaucratic Want of Public Spirit,”
that has particular reference to Ireland. 7'he Statist,
as far as we know, is nothing if not a staid and sober
paper, and one of the things that gives it pause is the
economic condition of Ireland and the decrease in Irish
population. Our. economic contemporary was anxious
to know to what extent Ireland lived on contributions
from Irish emigrants abroad to.their relatives at home.
Tt would, indeed, be very valuable information if it
could be obtained. The Statist attempted to collect
‘some information in this connection. 7he¢ Statist
applied to the Postmaster of the G.P.O., Dublin, for

Wo distinctly stated that we were engaged in a study of the

and porhaps we ought to have poiuted out that we kuew therc
\was no_distnction mado between remittances from settlers abroad
(o their friends at homo and other remittances. .B"" we took for
granted that the Dublin Post Office would recognise that probably
most small moncy orders, up to £3 or £4 say, would be re-
mittances from poor scttlers to their friends at home; and it
did not scem to us too great a labour to expect from the Dublin
Post Office that it should take the trouble to state the amount
of these small orders sent to Ireland in onc particular year. We
can understand, of course, the unwill'ngness of the young to
undortake unpaid labour, the value of which they do not appre-
ciate. We can sympathise, moreover, with the desire of the
ofcials of all ranks to lose nene of the time which they devate
to motoring, cycling, golfing, football, and o on. But while
recreation is an oxcellent thing, thére are stll more excellent
things which ought to be attended to, and surcly the higher
officials at all events ought to understand that it is their duty to
supply information which nobody clse can supply when the in-
formation is asked for a public purpcse by a person in a respon-
sible position like the editor of Z'hc Statist. We are afraid that
the I burcaucracy, like the Russian, looks upon itself as so
superior to the ordinary population that it resents anything
savouring in its eyes of intrusion into its business. It is accus-
tomed to deal in a high and mighty spirit with all the affairs of 1 :
the nation, and it is intolerant of any assumption that it is not
perfect in its way.

O

— o~ -

It tickles our sense of humour to observe a staid and
sober English weekly like 7e Statist knocking its head
against the stone wal of British Bureaucracy in Ire-
land. We are so accustomed to Dublin Castle in this }
counfry that wo scarcely ever make the mistake of 24
kuocking our head against it; we usually reserve our 3
strength for such rare occasions when we can knock i
its head with something strong and hard enough to ¥
make it wince. The Secretary of the G.P.O. in Dublin
is a Brilish importation by the name of Bgerton, but
we suppose he is so steeped in the traditions of Irish
Bureaucracy now that even the request of the English
Statist is a thing to be brushed aside in the grand
manner of an Irish Government official quartered on
this unfortunate and bleeding country,

Ou Monday next the report of the directors of the
Hibernian Bank will be submitted to the shareholders’
ordinary general meeting at one o’clock.  The net
profits for the half-year after providing for rebate on
bills, interest on deposits, and bad and doubtful debts,
amount to £22,676 11s. 11d. and £3,038 9s. 10d. is
the balance from last account. The dividend for the -~
half-year at the rate of 6 per cent., which it is pro-
posed to pay, will consume £15,000. The directors
have transferred £7,000 to the Reserve, which now
stands at £142,500. They recommend that £1,000 be
added to the Officers’, Superannuation Fund, and that
the balance, £2,715 1s. 9d., be carried to next account.

Aspirants for clerkships on the Great Sourface Rail-
way may elect to be examined in French, German or
Latin, but if they know Irish it is no use to them—that
is, no use for the purposes of passing the examination:
Yob when a passenger on the Great Sourface Railway
gives his name in Irish he has to suffer great incon-
veniences if the railway officia’ concerned is totally
ignorant of Irish. Over a year ago, our rcaders may
1'ememl_)er, the Great Sourface Railway could only
deal‘ w1b]1 a particular name and address in Irvish by
tracing it on to an .envelope. About Christmas time
a passenger was returning from ‘Cork to Dublin. He
had the reburn half of a ticket, for whioh he paid
17s. 3d. The ticket collector wanted 5s. 3d. excess,
as he alleged that the ticket was not available on bh
‘train leaying Cork at 3.30 p.m. The passenge!

Bt

* JhNUARY: 26, 1907,

~ his name and address; he gave them in Irish. Tho

r‘t"  collector refused to take them, and threatened, we

understand, to have the passenger “charged at Kings-
bridge.”” ~ However, the threats of this anti-Irish
railway, like its stock, are at a serious discount.

-The passenger was not awed. The refusal to take
the name and address in Irish took place before Blar-
ney Station - was reached, and between Blarney and
Kingsbridge: there were many arguments concerning
Fhe alleged folly of Irish, and of individuals attempt-
ing to fight a railway company. Incidentally it was
remarked that the passenger would be grey before
his time if he took his ideas from the LrabEr!

At Kingsbridge the passenger obliged his lordship,
the ticket collector, by going with him to the office of
his Excellency, the Superintendent, though the low-
down Irish hind had the presumption to think that
such an important case might have been taken before
his Imperial Majesty, Napoleon Dent himself.

The Superintendent, our readers may be surprised
to hear, was quite courteous, and accepied the name
and address in Irish, which had already been refused
by the collector. Up to the time of our hearing from
the passenger he had not received any communication
from the Great Sourface Railway. Perhaps the rail-
way has run out of its stock of tramsfer paper, and
was therefore unable to address a letter to the pas-
senger! Might we suggest that Napoleon, when he is
giving evidence before the Viceregal Commission,
should report this case in full. If he does, perhaps he
might mention that at Christmas, whilst a sizfeen day
return to Clare from Manchester was 21s., a len day
return from Dublin to Cork was 17s. 3d.—neither
tickets, we understand, were available on the limited
mail train, and no information is given as to which
train is the limited mail. It will, however be seen
the advantage the cross-Channel passenger, who comes
once a year, has over the Irish passenger who, perhaps,
is frequently travelling over the Great Sourface route.
For 21s. the L. and N. W. Railway brings the pas-
senger from Manchester to Holyhead by express train,
and from there to Dublin by steamer; the Great Sour-
face take him all the way to Clare, allow him stay there
sixteen days, and brings him back again. TFor 17s. 3d.
4 only take a passenger to Cork and back and
restrict him to certain trains. The Great Sourface
take an English passenger from Dublin to Clare and
back, and allow him sixteen days there, all for about
10s. 6d., very probably less than 10s. 6d., but they
charge an Irish passenger 17s. 3d. (as a Christmas con-
sideration it is only that sum), for taking him to Cork
and back, and they only allow him stay there eight or
ten days. Just like importing Napoleons, etc., it is
an advantage sometimes to be from England. It
would be no harm if Mr. Sexton asked a
few questions on this point at the Commission, as
it is evident that other ““‘through rates inwards’’ want
arranging besides those for goods traffic. Perhaps Mr.
- S:xton may have an opportunity of doing so when the
eyidence from the Commercial Travellers’ Association
is being given.

We take the following from a leading article in
the Irish Times on Mr. Sympathy Bryce’s recent

harangue: ab Newcastle-on-Tyne, in England :— Well,
asi to that, Mr; Bryce will permit us to remind him
that there-are:a good many Irishmen, and not the
worst: Irishmen either, who are proud to call them-
selves Britons,” and have done their part in making
the British name respected throughout the world.”
We suppose Mr. Algernon Locker, the imported editer
of Alf Foz, masquerading as an Irishman, calls ‘him-
self a Briton; probably Mike M‘Carthy, “the Catho-
lic,”” calls himself a Briton also!

Sir Horace Plunkett wrote in his book, “‘bu(;' tl'le
home of the strictly civic virtues and efficiencies is in
Protestant Ireland.”’ Apparently some of those who
may be expected to know Befast, which is a strong-
hold of what is called “ Protestant Ireland,”’” have mis-
givings concerning the quality and quantity of “civic
virtues”’ in the town of the bolts and nuts. A non-
Catholic minister, preaching from the pulpit of St.
George’s, High Street, Belfast, referred to—

The present disgraceful state of affairs prevailing in the city
of Belfast. What was tho present state of things? It had
actually come to this—that the honest men of Belfast had become
so hopeless that they had ceased to take any interest in municipal
affairs—he was, of course, speaking generally—and that, because
of their own indifference, it was almost impossible to get upright
and honourable men to come forward and represent them in the
Corporation.

This non-Catholic prcacher admitted that there were
some honest men in the Belfast Corporation, but he
said : —

But yet they could not avoid putting down to the Corporation
as a whole the charge of incapacity in managing civic affairs.
Our Corporation could not make a decent bit of road, or put a
good bit of macadam on the streets, but they all went to church
on Sunday and zaid their prayers as :f nobody objected.

What does Sir Horace think of this onslaught by a non-
Catholic preacher on portion of “the home of the
strictly civic virtues and efficiencies ’? We quote fur-
ther from this sermon as reported in the Belfast Even-
ing Telegraph : —

The Irish Roads Tmprovement Association had been trying for
some time to teach the Corporation how to put down macadam,
and there was certainly an improvement. But the waste of the
ratepayers’ money during the past few years was a scandal and a
shame. The Corporation could not put down a proper pieco of
wooden pavement. It did not require the use of a viagraph to
show them how the first portion was put down. If the members
of the Corporation only put the'r heads together, there would be
ths finest picce of wooden pavement, in Great Britain or Ireland.
But there was something worse than mere incapacity. Our city
had achieved a fame very much resembling infamy.

The preacher, who evidently looks to the East where
England ‘ies, for England’s good opinion, also said :—

I our Lord on beholding the city of Jerusalem from the Mount
of Olives wept, one might well weep for the city of Belfast. But
all was not hopeless or lost. Something st’ll remained to be
done, and they as honest men could do it. If only the honest
men of Belfast would unite thero were plenty of them to take
away the stigma attaching to the city of Belfast, so that when
travelling in England or el:ewhere one need no longer be ashamed
to say what city he came from.

Who are we to believe in these cross-current times? Sir
Horaco has said: “but the home of the strictly civic
virtues and officiencies is in Protestant Ulster,”” and
now comes along a non-Catholic clergyman telling his
Belfast Congregation:—*But the waste of the rate-

It is seldom we see a paper that calls itself the “Irish
Catholic.”” It is edited by a Mr. Dennchy, one of the
pioneers of Humphrey’s Dump, a gentleman. who was
editor of the Ha’penny Dreadful in the days when that
unhappy organ was the penny Platitude. Our readers,
we are sure, have a lively recollection of the revelations
that appeared in the LEADER concerning the Provincial
Bank. These revelations showed that corporation up
as one of the most exclusively “Saved ’ institutions in
the land.  There are few, if any, institutions more
sensitive to public opinion than banks, and if the
“TIdolators’’ turned a bold front to the Provincial
Bank, that instibution would soon listen to reason.
But it can laugh up its sleeve to some extent so long
as it has organs like the so-cal ed Irish Catholic to puff
it up. Mr. Dennehy’s paper got the ad. from the Pro-
vincial Bank, but that no doubt is only a detail. In
the course of an editorial note on the rather exclusive
Provincial Bank, we read: “It would be matter for
regret if this was not the case, because the Provincial
Bank deserves well. of our people ... . .”7 We
wonder did Mr. Dennehy of the * Irish Catholic >’ ever
hear of ““Three Railways and a Bank.”

In another editorial note this so-cal’ed “ Irish
Catholic > congratulates the Unionist and Irish T'imes
candidate, Mr. E. Bewley, on his victory in the South
City Ward (Dublin). The “Irish Catholic”’ says:—
“Mr. Bewley is a Protestant and a Unionist, but he
‘has pledged himself against the introduction of politics
at Corporation meetings, while he is a merchant of
enterprise, and generally a citizen of high repute. We
trust that Mr. Bewley’s wel -deserved success will have
the effect of encouraging other burgesses of similar
quality to make effort to release the Corporation from
the condition of disrepute to which it has been reduced
by the conduct of too many of its present members.”

Hero is an .editorial note in the so-called “Irish
Catholic,” the puffer of the Provincial Bank and the
advertiser of a Little Britain Street pub.:—

Tho King left London on Monday to join the Queen at Sand-
riigham, and the Prince of Wales also left on that day for his
residence at the same place. Their Majesties will remain .at
Sandringham until thoy return to Buckingham Palace to hold
tho usual Levees and Courts, for which rogulations have been
issued by the Lord Chamberlain, t

No doubt Mr. Dennehy has been invited to the
“usual Levees’’; and we presume that he will get his
court costume made to order in England. Papers like
this are not, to say the least, an unmixed blessing to
the Catholic Cause in Ireland; the forces that are
rising against Catholicity in Ireland call for something
far different from Mr. Dennéhy’s “Irish Catho ic,”” if
they are to be fought effectively.

Here is another “Irish Catholic’’ editorial par.:—

His Royal Highness Field Marshal the Duke of Connaught,
the Duchess of Connaught, the Princess Patricia of Connaught,
and the members of the Duke’s staff, arrived at Marsoilles at the
end of last week, and embarked immediately for the Tast on
board the P. and O. steamer Marmora. - The Duke will visit
Ceylon, the Straits Settlements, Hong Kong, and Egypt, examining
the local military arrangements.

What an unconsciously comic country this is, to-be
sure. Here is Mr. Dennehy talking of “tame’’ Catholics

—but they are far off—they are in Germany! The.
eyes of the fool are on the ends of the earth.  Mr.

Dennehy, no doubt, can spot a “ tame ’—in Germany ;
we do not accuse him of suffering from morbid intro-
spection. One editorial paragraph commences:—“The
‘tame’ Catholic has made his appearance in Germany,
just as he has often done in our midst.”’ However, is
it not recorded in story that the kettle called the sauce-
pan black! Happy Germany! The “Irish Catholic’’
says:—“ The poor, weak-kneed creature, however, is
not likely to find comfortable existence amongst the

THE INTERMEDIATE RULES (1908).

“ 1" HE publication of the correspondence between the

Intermediate Commissioners and the Irish
Government, arising out of the condemnation of the
1907 rules by vote of the House of Commons on May
21st, 1906, gives an additional interest to the recently
published rules for 1908.

The House of Commons by its vote practically
ordered (1) that Irish be put in a position of equality
with French and German, and (2) that Domestic
Economy be made a “main subject ”’ for girls. The
Irish Executive promptly brought the matter under
the notice of the Intermediae Board and requested
them to furnish a draft of new rules in compliance
with the views of the House. The Commissioners
turned the tables neatly and comp etely on Mr. Bryce.
It is amusing to find the Chief Secretary, who had such
a wonderful fund of sympathy, yet who could not find

" time to receive a deputation of Intermediate Head-

_badness of their cause.

of a motion of censure on himself and his Government.
Could even Mr. Bryce give a stronger argument for
Home Rule? The ensuing letters are most interesting.
The assertion of independence of the Castle on the part
of the Board excites some admiration, and would be
deserving of strong support were it not for the extreme
The Catholic Headmasters’
Association had repeatedly asked the Board to place

_ Irish on a footing of equality with French and German,

whether it was “possible and desirable’”’ to make "1;‘

and the Convent Schools’ Committee had asked that
Domestic Economy be added to the main or exhibition
subjects for girls. The Commissioners paid no heed to
the Headmasters, but after a'tussle with 8ir Antony
McDonne l, they agreed to give effect to.the wishes of

Parliament, in tthe rules of 1908. i B

Heads' of sohiools” were then askéd’ ‘by" the Board

a condition of passing the examination that candidates
should pass in two languages in addition to English.
Practicaly all, I believe, admitted the possibility,
but a big majority voted against the desirability.
I can only speak for myself in giving reasons. I did
nol consider it desirable to increase the number of
ob'igatory subjects; I could not see that a second lan-
guage was more desirable than a second mathematical
subject.

The New Rules require two languages; they also
require not merely two but three mathematical sub-
jects, at least for boys.

Domestic Economy and Hygiene is made a main
subject for girls, and apparently every candidate can
take as many of the special papers as he likes. Special
prizes for Greek and German are dropped.

The general standard of knowledge required for a
pass is raised very notably above that required by the
rules of the past few years.

The percentages required for passing in individual
subjects remain unaltered. The rule which comes to
the relief of a pupil who falls slightly below the 30 per
cent. in one subject remains. The insistence on the
30 per cent. in both English Literature and Composi-
tion which prevented the application of this beneficent
rule to the vital subject of English Composition and
Literature in 1906 is abolished.

But the conditions of passing the examination have
been altered substant’aly. The two years’ compulsory
Science, etc., is retained.

In Preparatory Grade the pupil must pass in (@)
English, (5) One of the languages—(1) Latin, (2)
French, (3) German, (4) Irish—(c) Mathematics, (d)
Experimental Science, (¢) One other subject.

In Junior, Middle and Senior Grades the require-
ments are (a) English, (5) Two of the following lan-
guages—(1) Greek, (2) Latin, (3) French, (4) German,
(5) Irish—(c) Mathematics, (d) One other subject
which, except for those exempt, must be Experimeatal
Science. N

The term Mathematics is defined by rule 34.—

“To pass in Mathematics, boys must obtain not less
than 30 per cent. of the marks allotted to each of the
two papers set in Preparatory and Junior Grades, and
in the Middle and Senior Grades 30 per cent. on the
paper in Arithmetic and Aflgebra, and 30 per cent. on
either Geometry or Trigonometry. Girls may piss in
Mathematics by obtaining 30 per cent. in Arithmetic
and Algebra or Geometry or Trigonometry.”’

The chief difference between the conditions of. passing
“in ‘Preparatory Grade in 1908 as compared with 1907,
is the substitution of Mathematics for Arithmetic or
Algebra—the substitution of Arithmetic, Algebra and
Geometry for one subject only—Arithmetic or Algebra.
The raising of the standard in Mathematics is very

idecided, and, in my opinion, very desirable.

For the Junior, Middle and Senior Grades a fairly
definite set of subjects takes the place of the loose
alternatives of the courses. In those, as in Prepara-
tory, there is a raising of the standard which is most
notable in the case of Mathematics.  Hitherto in
three of the four courses a candidate could pass with
a knowledge of Mathematics that wonld be represented
by 30 per cent. of the marks in Arithmetic or Algebra
or Geometry. The result was that in many schools
Geometry was never taught, and candidates for honours
were not troubled with the mere pass subject Arith-
‘metic. The new rules require every boy to get 30 per
cent. in Geometry (or Trigonometry) and 30 per cent. in
‘Arithmetic and Algebra. The marks assigned to this
‘ composite subject are divided thus:—

“the new rules is the extension of Mathematics and the
raising of Arithmetic from being a mere pass subject.
The other notable feature of the new -rules is the in-
stence in Junior, Middle and:Senior Grades on  a

I have no objection to the rule requiring two lan-

It may bo asked, why then did I not reply to the query |
of the Intermediate Board, say that it was desirable to- -
insist on the two languages? Well, in addition to the
reasons given above, I was influenced by the conviction 1‘
that the Board’s.query had some connection with the |
vote of the House of Commons; and I was reluctant to ,; 4
assist the Commissioners in getting out of a difficulty
that they got into by repeated’y flouting the expressed 1
views of our Association. I was unwilling to give my
opinion until the Board would have settled its difficulty
with Parliament. I say now that the two-language
rule will present no difficulty whatever to most schools.
As an illustration of this statement I may say that over
97 per cent. of the pupi's of my school passed in ab
least two languages, and if the two-language rule had {
been in force I need scarcely say that the few stragglers
would not have been allowed out of the Irish classes.
This rule ought to largely increase the number of
students taking Irish.

The net result of the new rules ought, therefore, to
be an educational improvement (1) by compeliing the
schools generally” to -teach Mathematics more exten-
sively, and (2) by inducing the schools to take up Irish
more general'y, and to teach it more thoroughly.

It has been said, by way of complaint, that the new
rules, by increasing the difficulty of securing a Pass,
will lead to the neglect of the backward pupi's. From
my acquaintance with boys of almost all degrees of in- :
telligence, I can state that ractically every boy, even | i
of mediocre ability, can be got to pass if the school i
has a sufficiently large staff of competent teachers. ;
The backward boys in backward schools are not so
numerous or so important that they are to determine 3

1
|

the standard of Intermediate education for the whole
country. y

By all means let suitable provision be made for
them, but not at the expense of, and to the detriment of, :
genuine Intermediate Education. Many of the evils |
of the Intermediate system of recent years are the out- 9
come of an attempt to adapt the same curriculum and &
programmes to the needs of Intermediate students
proper and of students who require to be trained for
clerkships merely. The attempt has been a greater
failure than most people are aware of. Let us hope
that the raising of the standard of Intermediate Edu-
cation will lead to the establishment of a Higher
Primary or Commercial Course. But that is touching
rather a large question.

My remarks have hitherto been confined to the
educational value and effect of the New Rules. Next
week, with your permission, I shall examine how far
they are a fair basis of distvibution of school grants
among the schools, and a fair basis of competition for
Medals, Exhibitions, etc., among the pupils.

URING the recent holidays I was taking a walk
one evening on our wi'd sea-coast lighted by
the pale gleam of the Christmas moon. On turning the
corner of a cliff I found myself suddenly blinded by a
brilliant glare of light. When my eyes became accus-
tomed to it, I saw that I was in the vestibule of the
Fairies’ Music Hall, and I heard a confused uproar
proceeding from within the closed folding-doors as if
a new turn was being greeted with unusual and wild
applause. My interest and curiosity were furiously
aroused. I approached the strange little being that
sat near the doors with a bundle of tickets in his hand
and a look of profound gravity on his face, and said
tentatively : «“ What shall T do? T have left my season:
ticket at home.”” ‘That’s ali right, sir,”’ he answered,
“pass right in, It’s Christmas times anyway.”
Though puzzled by his reference to the season, I was
much gratified by this gracious permission, and my
attention was all absorbed in the brilliant scene that
burst upon my astonishéd eyes as soon as I had entered

the hall. &
The applause that had greeted the “new turn,” at
his entrance, on the stage was dying away, and I could

That riding breeches are vesture which is

AN EARTEQUAKE IN DUBLIS.

$¢7 T would take an earthquake to settle the Irish

I Land Question,’” saidqxv. distingushed politician
to Parnell some twenty-five years ago.  “Then we
must have an Earthquake,’’ replied the great leader.
And we had.  Metaphorically, of course, be it under-
stood, yet if we let our fancy stray, and trifle for a

- moment with the thought of thoso seismotic disturb-

ances which have wrought such sad havoc in distant
lands, we may evolve some quaint ideas as to what
might pos_sll:r.ly be the outcome in our own island were
we the.vwt.xms of a similar disaster. [The weather-
clerk will kindly note that I am not praying for such
? cond]lblon of things. I am but toying with a playful
fancy.

 Fudt Tllium Dublin, once one of the fairest cities
in Europe, now lies a heap of smouldering ruins!
De_str_ucbiou is manifest on all sides. Those public
buildings, those stately dwellings, the wide thorough-
fares, the spacious squares so eloquently described in
every geography and gazetteer, no longer exist to
delight the eye, or to strengthen the argument of
those who claimed that Dublin was a capital. A con-
fusion of bricks and stones is all that is left as the
wayfarer cautiously picks his steps through the débris.
So this is College Green! That dismantled ruin yonder
is all that remains of Trinity College.  Shades of
Mahaffy, has it come to this? Dear me, was that
heap once the Bank of Ireland? Once the old Parlia-
ment House? Did Grattan thunder there, and Flood?
Did Hogg and Pim discuss the Bank Rate in that
House of Lords, and defy Bryce to put them

out “of it? Does yonder mass of stones in the
dim distance represent all that is left of the
City Hall? Where be your gibes now? Loyal

addresses will no longer be debated in your walls.
Auditors will not be denounced or. refused the City
Books. And this malodorous pile of bricks and stones
and rubbish-—can this be The Castle? The proud
fortalice whence a hundred viceroys have issued to
Howth or Kingstown; the headquarters of the Garri-
son since the days of John. Dust and ashes, over-
~thrown at last!

Such are the natural reflections on the morrow of
the great disaster. But more far-reaching conse-
quences may follow an upheaval such as: that
described, affecting not merely city life, but the wel-
fare of the whole country. Trinity College is gone.
The splendid buildings, ‘the ancient halls associated
with memories and traditions which endeared them to

. their owners, and aroused the anger, aye and the
. envy, of those excluded from them, are things of yes-

terday. - The famous library, ever a fruitful source of
bitter controversy, no Ionger remains to be a
stumbling-block to those who seek a settlement of the
question. The College Chapel is no more. The

~ Bishops, tho, Jesuits, the Fottrells, of the day on the

one hand; the Traills, the Mahaflys, the Dowdens,
on the other, have & new situation to deal with. The
edifice, steeped jn- centuries of Elizabethan atmos-
phere, has- vanished from the scene.

' The “Old House” in College Green has mot with
the fate which long before befell ‘the Parliament that
sat within its walls. Even as the Coliseum is linked

- with the destiny of Rome, so while stands the House

of Parliament Home Rule shall stand, or, at least,
the demand for it. Generation of Irishmen followed

- on generation, striving for the restoration of the Irish

Parliament to the Old House in College Green. It

- has become the Mecca of the Irish race, or, at any rate,
- of the greater portion of it.

The picture of the win-
dowless building roused as patriotic emotions as that
of Robert Emmet speaking from the dock. No longer
will that picture adorn the leaders of the Freeman’s
Journal. No longer will a reference to those walls bo
inc'uded in the peroration of an after-dinner speech.

“Sinn Fein men will rejoice because they know that a

powerful link has snapped in the chain which ‘binds
their countrymen to the Home Rule idea. Parlia-
mentarians will mourn, still perchance, may feel that
Unionist opposition will be less strong since a Parlia-
ment in the “ Old House’ is a dream that now can
never be realised.

Stopping carefully over the prostrate forms of
Grattan and William III., now lying in the dust and
none so poor to do them insult, one comes to Cork
Hill, deserted now like that of Tara, and crowned
with tho ruins of its former glories. The voice that
once made the rafters ring will wake echoes here no
more. The local humourists will cause no future mer-
riment by writing “Haul” for “Hall.” The fate
of monarchs will not tremble in the balance in the
Council Chamber, nor will the unemployed vary the
monotony of their drab existences by watching .the
City Fathers in the discharge of their paternal duties.
At least, they will not do so in the hall so long the
scene of civic labours. A force more violent than
irate ratepayers has swept the hall away.

The Castle is no more. Sad news this, my masters.
Breathe it not in Belfast; whisper it not in the streets
of London. That strange conglomeration of buildings
—a palace, a chapel, a keep, a police barrack, a public
office—is now one mass of smouldering ruins. The
fortress which had resisted attacks from without and.
undermining from within—Land, National, and
United Irish Leagues, Dunravenism and Devolu-
tion, abolitionists and reformers—has at last suc~
cumbed. True, indeed, the forces which sheltered
within its walls still exist to carry on their work. The
Lord Lieutenant, in the seclusion of the Viceregal
Lodge, has escaped the violence of the earthquake,
and as the disturbance has not been felt in East
Ulster or in England, the genius of the Castle, so to
speak, may not have suffered. Yet The Castle is gone,
and another glowing phrase has departed from the
vocabulary of the political speaker. The necessity to
live up to a tradition has gone, too. A new start in
new surroundings must be made. The old “files,”
the old records, have been destroyed, and the handi-
cap of ancient precedent exists no longer.

It is a quaint and perhaps an idle fancy. Two
ideas, however, suggest themselves, but lest they may
fail in their timidity to force themsclves upon the
reader’s notice, it may be weli to indicate them.

Such is the force of traditions and associations that not
infrequently the central principles underlying the
questions on which men join issue are forgotten, and.
the standard or the battle-cry becomes more important
than the vital cause for which the fight is waged.
Again, it took an Earthquake to settle temporarily
the Land Question; will anything less settle the
National one? Avis.

TWO LECTURES ON IRISH MUS.C.

AST week brought quite a stir in the Irish music
question.  On the previous Saturday, Mr.
Stephen Gwynn had an interesting article on the
Journal of the Irish Folk Song Society, while in the

week itself came a re-delivery of Mr. Robert
O’Dwyer’s lecture, before the National Literary
Society, with musical illustrations by Mr. Arthur

Darley, and a lecture at the Molesworth Hall, with
many musical illustrations, delivered by the Rev.
Canon Healy, LL.D., Rector of Kells. I would sug-
gest that Mr. O’Dwyer should publish his lecture in
pamphlet form, or deliver it yet again in some central
public hall, with a full supply of musical illustra-
tions, vocal as well as instrumental. In the lecture
before the National Literary Society, Mr. O’Dwyer
repeated his former views with no diminution of
courage or emphasis. He protested against the notion
that “real’ Irish music is exclusively represented by
the airs that survive amongst untaught peasants in
remote country places. He allowed, of course, that
amongst some of these peasants there are musical
remains of real beauty and value; “but,”’ said he,
“let them not make the fatal error of imagining that
those remnants represented in any sense the highest -
form of Irish musical art, or that the art of the
renowned bards of Eire was no more nor better than
that expressed by the so-called traditional singer or
player of to-day.” That is exactly my own conten-
tion in this matter; better expressed, no doubt, but
exactly the same position. My firm belief is thaf
whatever is peculiar, odd, or strange about any of the
airs of the traditional peasant-singer is merely an
exceptional thing, and not a dominating or charac-
teristic feature of Irish music as a whole. I agree,
too, with the lecturer’s view, that “the backwaxd
state of Irish music is almost entirely due to the chill-
ing-influence of the idea that all culture in music is
foreign to the spirit of our Irish inheritance.” and to
the notion “that all modern music was in deadly
opposition to it.”” ;

In the Rev. Canon Healy’s lecture, our old friend,
the “ gapped scale,”” turned up once more. Among
-the musical illustrations of the lecture was one intended
to exhibit this scale, and stated to omit the fourth and
seventh notes of the scale—I presume the sub-domi-
nant and the leading-note; a sufficiently extraordi-
nary scale, certainly. Perhaps some of those who
oppose any possible development of Irish music do not
quite realise what an adherence to such a scale
involves. It makes harmony, and therefore all con-
certed music, impossible. With such a scale the fol-
lowing important chords in harmony would be impos-
sible: common chord of the dominant; dominant
seventh ; common chord of relative minor of the domi-
nant; common chord of sub-dominant, and of its rela-
tive minor. This will surely show that the art of “ the
untutored peasant,’’ as one traditionalist calls him, con-
tains elements which make musical progress impossible
—unless, indeed, the omitted notes are only to be
omitted in the melody, not in the harmony, a pro-
posal which some will promptly denounce as barbarous.
For my own part, I imagine that the non-use of the
omitted notes just “ happened,” and is not the result
of any special design; that it was fortuitous, not tho
outcome of any special intention; the same I should
suppose to have been the case with some other features
of this traditional music. An English poet once wrote
what he called a “song without a sibilant.”” In that
case, no doubt, the omission of the “s’’ and “z’’ was
intentional ; but would anybody thence argue that his
alphabet must have had only twenty-four letters? I
have seen a sonnet, and a rather good one, written in
monosyllables, expressly to vindicate the powers and
virtues of “the short word.”” How would it do, in
ages to come, to contend that the writer of it knew no

come down to us. “They were lost,”as they had never

reed is tradition to rely upon for the permanent pre-
sorvation of music? The Rev. Canon said that “from
the description of Cambrensis, there' must be harmony
of some kind oven in the modern sense,” in
the Irish music of that day a declaration
which must be rather staggering to the people who
won’t have Irish music harmonised at any price. The
lecturer also said there must have been a large number
of instruments in angient Ireland, adding that twenty
were mentioned by our old writers, hardly any of
which are not known. How little can we rely on
tradition for the preservation of anything, even of
such tangible things as instruments! With - the
Canon’s strictures on Moore, as respects the melodies,
I quite agree.

Returning to the name of Cambrensis, it seems to
me that if the encomium of that writer be true, “that
in music the Irish were beyond all others,”” then the
Irish of that day must have had the same musical
cu'ture, as regards the harp, at all events, was then
prevailed elsewhere. In later days we know that men
like Carolan, Hempson, Arthur O’Neill, and their
compeers, -possessed such training—all of which is
much out of tune with the proposal to regard “ the
untutored peasant’’ as the human basis of Irish
musical art.

There have always been disputes over questions of
music, and there always will. Mozart was the victim
of condemnation for his innovations; so was Beethoven,
and so was Wagner. Only the other day Richard
Strauss was defeated in his candidature for admission
to the Berlin Academy of Arts. All the poets,
painters, and architects voted for him; all the musi-
cians against him! Let us see tc it that musical dif-
ferences do not prevent musical progress in Ireland.
